Autoradiography of specimens from the graphite of capsule 36 (from which the $\mathbf{F}_2$ -deficient fuel was removed by melting) also showed activity concentrated in a thin layer on the outer surface of the graphite; however, the graphite from this capsule showed a faint, but definite, penetration of activity halfway to the center.

A barely credible theory is that the large quantity of radiolytic fluorine produced after its removal from the reactor generated substantial amounts of a fission product fluoride, which volatilized into the graphite during the salt meltout procedure used only for capsule 36.

A closer resolution of the uranium distribution was obtained from x radiography by a technique (recently developed in the ORNL Metals and Ceramics Division)<sup>7</sup> in which the interference from fission product activity is negligible.
The main deposit of uranium penetrated the graphite to a depth of less than $0.05 \, \text{mm}$ ; however, there were also fringed proliferations that extended perhaps three times as deep.

Under the metallographic microscope, there were no signs of radiation damage, chemical alteration, penetration of salt, nor even of the $\sim 3$ wt % concentration of uranium that apparently existed in a 2-mil layer at the surface of the graphite.

This 0.l-mil-thick film, which almost certainly arose when this particular specimen was heated to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under vacuum to recover any residual xenon, was identifiable only as probably metallic from its metallographic appearance.

However, the container from the $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ bakeout was stainless steel; and in accord with recent results on the volatility of components from stainless steel, the film may well have been volatilized manganese and chromium.

The one case of graphite damage observed in ORNL-MTR-47-4 is therefore ascribed to fluorine attack either during the postirradiation period before gas sampling or during startups after reactor shutdowns in the course of the in-pile operation.

During the next 1271 hr, the pressure increased.

After further buildup of pressure during irradiation at $110^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the temperature was increased to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , resulting in a steady pressure decline until the original (helium) pressure was reached.
The third sample was withdrawn (hour 4412), and the autoclave was purged.
The sample contained essentially pure helium ( $\sim 100$ ppm $\mathbf{F}_2$ , no $\mathrm{CF_4}$ , etc.).

Further experiments were then conducted at temperatures between 110 and $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the presence and absence of the source in order to examine generation and recombination effects, and a final sample was taken.
The generation of $\mathbf{F}_2$ appeared to require a substantial induction period, originally and after complete (sample 3, $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) recombination.
Similar induction periods and some production of $\mathbf{F}_2$ have been reported, in the case of neutron-irradiated LiF, as summarized by Billington and Crawford.[18]
In our experiment the threshold appeared at a specific dose of 2.7 x $10^{22}$ ev absorbed per gram.
This is somewhat more than an order of magnitude less energy than appeared to be the threshold for various effects in the neutron irradiation experiments cited above.[18]

The generation of $\mathbf{F}_2$ appeared to reach a steady-state rate of about $6+$ psi per 1000 hr, corresponding to a $\mathbf{G}_{\mathbf{F}_2}$ of 0.05 molecule of $\mathbf{F}_2$ per 100 ev absorbed (0.03 to 0.07).

Such values of $\mathbf{G}_{\mathbf{F}_2}$ are consistent with the previous in-pile capsule experiments.[15] At temperatures below $110^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the rate appeared to increase somewhat with temperature, but data are inadequate for a firm conclusion.
The gas recombined completely at $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and appeared roughly to balance production and recombination at $130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Increases in temperature from 110 to $130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and from 130 to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the absence of irradiation each appeared to increase the recombination rate about threefold.

No fluorine evolution was detected in two experiments with LiF in which the total doses were 1.4 and 2.6 (x $10^{18}$ ) Mev/g and the dose rates were 8.6 x $10^{16}$ and 1.6 x $10^{17}$ Mev hr $^{-1}$ g $^{-1}$ .
This uptake amounted to 4 to 6 $\mu$ g per 8.3 x $10^{16}$ Mev/g in one experiment and 10 to 20 $\mu$ g per 8.3 x $10^{16}$ Mev/g near the end of the other, lower dose-rate, experiment.

The differences between the initial rates of fluorine evolution in experiment 9 and those in the previous, higher intensity experiments 4 and 5a are considered significant and evidence that, up to a dose of 4 to 5 (x $10^{17}$ ) $\mathrm{Mev/g}$ , the $\mathbf{G}_{\mathbf{F}_2}$ values increase with decreasing dose rate within the dose rate range investigated.
As previously suggested,[19] the results of the final two exposures in experiment 4 indicate a similar effect.
The effect of dose rate on $\mathbf{G}_{\mathbf{F}_2}$ at doses above about 5 to 6 (x $10^{17}$ ) $\mathrm{Mev/g}$ cannot be evaluated from the data.
The changes in evolution rate with increasing dose observed in experiment 9 have led to a reconsideration of the data for experiment 5a.
It was previously believed[19] that the evolution rate had reached an approximate steady-state value before the experiment was terminated.
It now seems likely that further changes in the rates would have occurred in both experiments 5a and 4 if the radiation exposures had been continued.
Although $G_{F_2}$ values found in this work are in the neighborhood of those found by others in in-pile capsule experiments,[20] they are significantly lower than some of the reported values.

They are also less than some of those found by Compere and co-workers[21] in the exposure of MSRE-type salt to cobalt gamma rays, but about the same as the maximum $G_{F_2}$ values reported by Rainey and co-workers[22] for x-ray irradiation of the salt.

The initial dose rate in experiment 9 was about $4 \times 10^{16}$ Mev g $^{-1}$ hr $^{-1}$ , while those in the gamma ray[21] and x-ray[22] work were about $4.5 \times 10^{13}$ and $8 \times 10^{12}$ .
The maximum doses in the latter experiments were about $3 \times 10^{17}$ and $7 \times 10^{15}$ Mev/g.
By use of the experiment labeled $65 \, \text{w/cm}^3$ in Fig.

20, for comparison and example, it can be estimated from the data given in the figure that the intensities in the salt in this capsule were about $8 \times 10^{14}$ , $5 \times 10^{14}$ , and $4 \times 10^{14}$ Mev g $^{-1}$ hr $^{-1}$ after 11.4, 23.4, and 38.4 days of cooling.
It can also be estimated that the doses at the same times were about $4 \times 10^{17}$ , $6 \times 10^{17}$ , and $7 \times 10^{17}$ Mev/g.

The reported $G_{F_2}$ values were as high as 0.07 in the cobalt irradiation.[21] For the capsule in-pile at temperatures of 30 to $36^\circ C$ , they were about 0.04 from about 11 to 40 days but were below 0.04 at the later times.[20]

In general, the results of the electron irradiations of MSRE salt appear consistent with the kinetic picture of salt radiolysis suggested by Blankenship and co-workers.[20] However, the results of the present work suggest that the rates of their proposed diffusion and recombination steps are probably dependent upon radiation intensity.

The occurrence of a maximum in the evolution rate curve of experiment 9 is an additional indication that back reaction with defects (produced by the loss of fluorine) in the MSRE fuel is an important factor in limiting the amount of fluorine evolved.

In addition, or alternatively, the increase in crystallite size which has been reported[19] for some Van de Graaff samples may also be important in reducing the rate of fluorine production and/or diffusion to the surface.
The increase in fluorine-evolution rates which occurred after a dose of about $10^{18}$ MeV/g suggests that some marked change occurred in the salt in this dose range.

Little can be said about the absence of fluorine evolution from LiF under irradiation in this work except to note that this finding agrees with the x-ray irradiation result of Rainey and co-workers $^{22}$ and confirms their suggestion that fluorine evolution under irradiation differs markedly for different salts.

Commercial $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ frequently contains approximately $1\%$ water, which is difficult to remove by usual methods; the solubility of $\mathrm{BeO}$ in melts at elevated temperatures is low (approximately $1000~\mathrm{ppm}$ at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

Determinations of the melting point of $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ have been made at this laboratory by several means: (1) by thermal gradient quenching of the pure materials, (2) by extrapolation of $\mathrm{BaF}_2$ - $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ liquidus points obtained from quenching data,
and (3) by direct observation of the melting-freezing behavior under helium atmospheres containing less than $100\mathrm{ppm}$ $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ .
In the two former experiments, the melting point was determined to be $548^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

In the latter experiments, the material began to soften at approximately $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , melted to a clear, viscous fluid which remained clear and transparent to temperatures as high as $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and retained its clear glassy appearance during subsequent cooling to room temperature.

For these reasons, and because the stereochemistry of metal ions displaying eightfold coordination is currently of interest, $^{20}$ a complete crystal-structure analysis of $6\mathrm{LiF} \cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2 \cdot \mathrm{ZrF}_4$ was undertaken.
This substance is tetragonal, having a centrosymmetric unit cell of dimensions $\mathsf{a}_{\mathsf{o}} = 6.57$ A, $\mathsf{c}_{\mathsf{o}} = 18.62$ A; the space group is I4₁/amd - D₁₉₉.
Both the $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$ ions have discrete coordination polyhedra.
The beryllium ion is surrounded by four equidistant fluoride ions at 1.57 A, forming a nearly regular tetrahedron.
In this respect the beryllium coordination is similar to that in $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ ,[21] another primary phase in the LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ system.

About the zirconium ion there are four fluoride ions at 2.07 A and four at 2.16 A arranged as two interpenetrating tetrahedra with a common center; these eight fluoride ions form an irregular dodecahedron.

This type of eightfold coordination is common for early transition-metal ions,[20] including that of the zirconium ion in $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{ZrF}_6$ .[22] The other eight-coordination polyhedron frequently observed is the square antiprism exhibited by $\mathrm{ZrF}_4$ itself.[23]

The sparging times required were, nonetheless, only 20 to 40 hr.
Of the total amount of sulfur added in the three tests, $\sim 90\%$ has been recovered as $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ ; $\sim 93\%$ apparently could have been removed by extended sparging.
The remaining $7\%$ ( $\sim 70$ mg of sulfur), presumably lost by corrosion reactions, is thought to have been deposited principally in the cool parts of the exhaust line.

As discussed previously, it is desirable either to maintain the ratio of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S} / \mathrm{H}_2$ in the gas phase below that required for NiS formation ( $\sim 0.001$ at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) or to maintain the temperature below $645^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to avoid formation of the Ni-NiS eutectic thought to be responsible for sulfide embrittlement of nickel.

As yet, insufficient data have been collected to explain the curious rate behavior observed in these tests: (1) an initial constant rate of $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{S}$ evolution which is proportional to the $\mathsf{H}_2$ flow but independent of the HF flow or the sulfur content; and (2) a logarithmic decrease in the $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{S}$ evolution rate, the half-time of which appears to be affected only by the $\mathsf{H}_2$ flow rate.

Nickel sulfide is a possible such solid since, during the period of constant $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ -evolution rate, the $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S} / \mathrm{H}_2$ ratio is only about twice that reported<sup>40</sup> for the equilibrium
at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
If the sulfide is dissolved, the previously saturating solid (presumably BeS or NiS) yields a sulfide concentration of $\sim 600$ ppm ( $\sim 0.02$ mole/kg).

This is in apparent disagreement with the last reported experiment involving beryllium reduction, wherein the maximum dissolved sulfide concentration appeared to be $\sim 100$ ppm ( $\sim 0.003$ mole/kg) at the same temperature.
However, a filtered sample taken early in the first of the present tests showed $820$ ppm total sulfur.

Perhaps the most important implication of the present results is that when sulfate was reduced to sulfide prior to its removal, the maximum $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S} / \mathrm{H}_2$ ratio attained in the exit gases was of the order of 0.0005.

This in turn indicates that for a given initial sulfur content, a fairly large volume of sparging $\mathrm{H}_2$ would be required; for example at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ it appears that at least 1500 std liters of $\mathrm{H}_2$ are required to remove $1\mathrm{g}$ of sulfur from 2LiF-BeF $_2$ as $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ .

The $\mathrm{H}_2$ volume indicated by this condition is a lower limit since the logarithmic behavior of the $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ evolution rate below 600 ppm would, of course, require considerably larger volumes.

At temperatures higher than $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , sulfur should be removed more rapidly because both the limiting $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S} / \mathrm{H}_2$ ratio should increase and the half-times of the latter removal stages should be lower.
Some 26,560 lb of fused fluoride mixtures will be required for one complete reactor loading.

The first production mixture is to consist of 15,300 lb of the mixture $\mathrm{Li^{7}F - BeF_{2}}$ (66-34 mole %) to be used as coolant- and flush-salt mixtures in prenuclear test operation of the reactor system.

These mixtures will be chemically identical but will differ in $\mathrm{Li^{6}}$ concentration; raw material batches having the highest $\mathrm{Li^{6}}$ concentrations will be used to make the coolant-salt mixture.
Approximately 5800 lb of coolant mixture will be prepared to fill a required volume of about 42 ft $^3$ .
The required volume of flush salt is about 73 ft $^3$ , for which the remaining 9500 lb of binary salt mixture will be prepared.
These production estimates include a calculated $10\%$ excess to allow for the usual contingencies.

The reactor fuel-salt mixture will be made from a $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ZrF_{4}}$ solvent mixture to which $\mathrm{UF_4}$ will be added as the eutectic mixture $\mathrm{LiF - UF_4}$ (73-27 mole %).

The overall composition of the fuel-salt mixture will be $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ZrF_{4} - UF_{4}}$ (65.0-29.1-5.0-0.9 mole %); fissionable $\mathrm{U^{235}}$ will comprise about one-third of the total uranium content.
Some 11,260 lb of the fuel mixture will be prepared for the first reactor loading of 73 ft $^3$ .

During 1963, approximately 470 kg of fluoride mixtures containing LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ was prepared, by operation of the large production facility, for chemical research programs and experimental engineering tests related to the molten-salt research program.

An additional 435 kg of the mixture NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ (37.5-37.5-25.0 mole %) was prepared in this facility for use by the ORNL Chemical Technology Division in pilot-plant studies of reactor fuel reprocessing schemes.
A total of 120 kg of various fluoride mixtures was prepared in smaller batches for use by other ORNL groups and for subcontract work on corrosion at the Battelle Memorial Institute.
Fluoride mixtures are prepared from high-quality fluoride salts in two batch-processing units, each having a capacity of about $2\mathrm{ft}^3$ .

Anhydrous HF was mixed with the helium sweep gas while heating to about $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to convert LiOH, either initially present or formed by pyrohydrolysis, to LiF; otherwise, heating to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a helium stream would permit the LiOH to be fused with the LiF to give an intractable mass.

The assembly $^{42}$ consists of a graphite cell approximately 2 in. in diameter and $4 - 1 / 2$ in. long which contains the melt; a quartz jacket approximately $2 - 1 / 2$ in. in diameter and 10 in. long with a removable cap for maintaining a controlled atmosphere over the melt; a 3-in.-ID resistance furnace; a furnace-temperature control; and a melt-temperature monitoring system.

A pyrolytic-graphite electrode $^{43}$ (PGE) coupled with a platinum quasi-reference electrode comprised the measuring system, while a third platinum electrode isolated in a separate porous-graphite inner compartment of the main cell served as a counter electrode.
In previous works $^{43,44}$ the PGE was prepared by brazing an 18-gage platinum wire directly to pyrolytic graphite at high temperature under vacuum.
The graphite was then machined to a 1/8-in. diameter, and a boron nitride insulating sheath was pressed to fit around the graphite.
A sample of MSRE fuel solvent having the composition given in Table 6.8 was loaded into the graphite electrolytic cell and was fused.
